edge: WTAL learns to detect and classify action instances with only category labels.
CBP is widely adopted to generate video features for action localization.
The optimization objectives between classification and localization result in temporally localized results suffering from the serious incomplete issue.
The paper considers distilling free action knowledge from VLP.
Vanilla VLP has an over-complete issue in its localization results.
The complementary nature of CBP and VLP results are fused to address the incomplete issue.
The framework consists of two branches acting as CBP and VLP, optimized through a dual-branch alternate training strategy.
Background pseudo-labels are distilled from the CBP branch during the B step.
Foreground pseudo-labels are distilled from the VLP branch during the F step.
The complementarity between the two branches is fused to promote one strong alliance.
The method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods based on experiments and ablation studies on THUMOS14 and ActivityNet1.2.
Temporal action localization (TAL) aims to localize actions in time.
TAL aims to localize and classify action instances from untrimmed long videos.
WTAL setting involves only video-level category labels for action localization.
CBP is used for video feature extraction in training action classifiers.
VLP results in confusion between action and background, leading to high true positives and false positives.
The framework collaboratively distills foreground from VLP and background from CBP, promoting mutual collaboration for more accurate results.
Methods using CBP suffer from incompleteness issue of detecting sparse discriminative action frames and high false negatives.

The main reason for high false negatives is that the optimization objective of classification pre-training focuses on finding discriminative frames for action recognition, which is different from the objective of complete localization.
Features from CBP are biased towards partial discriminative frames due to the lack of complete localization objective.
Many efforts have been made to solve the incompleteness issue but are often trapped in a 'performance-cost dilemma', where digging from barren category labels is chosen to keep costs low.
Lacking location labels fundamentally limits the performance in action detection.
The paper intends to jump out of the 'performance-cost dilemma' by raising the question of leveraging free action knowledge for complete detection results while maintaining cheap annotation overhead.
VLP has been successful in learning joint visual-textual representations from web data, providing rich information about objects, human-object interactions, and object-object relationships.
Learned representations from VLP could offer human-object co-occurrence priors, enhancing action localization.
This paper aims to fill the research gap by extracting action priors from VLP (specifically CLIP) to address the incompleteness issue in action detection.
VLP is distilled from CLIP to solve the incomplete issue for WTAL.
VLP is used to evaluate the temporal localization performance by frame-wise classification.
VLP results in high false positives due to confusing multiple action instances into a whole.
VLPs are trained using image-text pairs which lack sufficient temporal knowledge.
VLP relies on human-object co-occurrence for localization, struggling to distinguish actions with visually similar background contexts.
VLP struggles to distinguish actions with visually similar background contexts.
VLP struggles to distinguish actions due to visually similar background contexts.
CBP and VLP have complementary properties: CBP localizes high true negatives but serious false negatives, while VLP has high true positives but serious false positives.
Background knowledge is distilled from the CBP branch to leverage complementarity.
Foreground knowledge is distilled from the VLP branch to leverage complementarity.
The design rationale is to distill background knowledge from the CBP branch, while foreground knowledge from the VLP branch, for strong alliances.
We first warm up the CBP branch using only category supervision to initialize confident background frames.
We leverage high-quality pseudo-labels for the CBP branch to tackle the incomplete issue.
During B step, we distill background pseudo-labels for the VLP branch to solve the over-complete issue, hence obtaining high-quality foreground pseudo-labels.

Frames are classified based on scores, with scores more than δh and less than δl considered as foreground, while scores less than δl or with the wrong action category are considered as background.
Frames are also classified based on the correct action category, with correct category frames classified as foreground and frames with the wrong action category as background.

Comparing to various fusion strategies, the alternating strategy produces more precise and complete results.
The alternating strategy plays a similar role as multi-view co-training, where different components like CBP and VLP are utilized.
The multi-view co-training strategy treats CBP and VLP branches as two distinctive views, making it robust to pseudo-label noises.
In step B, the VLP branch is prompted for high-quality pseudo-labels to handle frames with conflicting predictions.
Feature contrastive loss is used to make results more discriminative in step F for further denoising.
At testing time, results from the CBP branch are leveraged for post-processing, as vision-language pre-training cannot handle Optical Flow.

node_2: video-level category probabilities and frame-level localization scores
edge: For action classification, classes with probability greater than θcls are selected, and for localization, detection scores are thresholded with θloc.
Consecutive snippets are concatenated as action proposals for localization, eliminating redundant proposals with soft non-maximum suppression.
THUMOS14 dataset consists of 413 untrimmed videos from 20 categories.
ActivityNet1.2 covers 9682 videos belonging to 100 categories.
Almost all the videos are single-category.
Action regions take up more than half of the video duration.
We train on 4619 training videos.
We evaluate on 2383 validation videos.
To evaluate localization performance, we follow the standard protocol to use mean Average Precision (mAP) at different intersections over union (IoU) thresholds.
To clearly evaluate the quality of pseudo-labels, we also report mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) averaged over the foreground categories and the background category.
To handle the large variety in video durations, we randomly sample T consecutive snippets for each video.
We utilize the TV-L1 algorithm to extract optical flow from RGB data.
For the CBP branch, we use Transformer architectures (multi-head self-attention, layer norm, and MLPs) as the backbone network.
For the VLP branch, we use a 2-layer temporal Transformer, prepend and append 16 prompt vectors to textual inputs, both are initialized by N (0, 0.01).
Both CLIP image encoder and text encoder are adopted.
Both CLIP image encoder and text encoder are initialized by N(0, 0.01), indicating they have a common initialization process.
The framework is optimized by Adam with the learning rate of 10^-4, signifying Adam plays a crucial role in optimizing the framework.
All hyper-parameters are set by grid search, showing that grid search is used to determine the values of hyper-parameters.
Pseudo-label thresholds δh = 0.3, δl = 0.1 and the balancing ratio λ = 0.05 are key components in setting up the framework.
For fair comparisons, CO2-Net [18] and DELU [7] were reproduced using both I3D and CLIP features, illustrating a connection between I3D and CLIP.
